PRESENTATION IX
CONSTRUCTIBLE BEAMS
COHOMOLOGY OF AN ALGEBRAIC CURVEby M. Artin
Summary

0. Introduction
1. The exit of the torsion beams
2. Buildable bundles
3. Kummer and Artin-Schreier theories
4. Case of an algebraic curve
5. The trace method.
0. Introduction

It will be noted that from this presentation, unlike the presentations precedents and the classical cohomological theory of topological spaces, we are obliged, for the validity of the essential statements, to limit ourselves to the torsion beams (and often even, more precisely, to torsion beams first with residual characteristics).

Let us point out that in fact a "reasonable" theory of cohomology integer (or even real) coefficients, for varieties over a field algebraically closed with characteristic \(p \neq 0\), analogous to the classical theory for the case \(k = \mathbb{C}\), does not exist (as noted by Serre). More precisely, there is no of contravariant functor \(H^1\), defined say on the category of projective schemes smooth on the body \(K\) alg. enclosed by car. \(p > 0\), with values in category finite-dimensional vector spaces on \(\mathbb{R}\) (or a subfield of \(\mathbb{R}\)), "commuting to products", i.e. such that \(H^1(X \times Y) \xrightarrow{\sim} H^1(X) \times H^1(Y)\), and such that \(\dim H^1(X) = 2\) if \(X\) is a connected curve of genus \(1\). In fact, he would follow that if \(X\) is an abelian variety on \(k\), then the map \(u \mapsto u^1\) of \(\operatorname{End}(X)\) in \(\operatorname{End}(H^1(X))\) is additive, therefore a representation of the opposite ring of \(\operatorname{End}(X)\). But in characteristic \(p\), there exist curves ellipticals \(X\) having as ring of endomorphisms a maximum order \(A\) of one quaternion algebra defined on \(\mathbb{Z}\) ([3], p.198), and such an algebra does not have obviously no representation in a 2-dimensional vector space on \(\mathbb{R}\), since \(A \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{R}\) is a body (the quaternion body).

pages.title: 1. The exit of the torsion beams

blocks.html: 1. The exit of the torsion beams

Let \(T\) be a topos and \(F\) be an abelian sheaf on \(T\). We can multiply by \(n\) in \(F\) (\(n \in \mathbb{Z}\) an integer), this multiplication being induced by the analogous operation in \((\mathrm{Ab})\). Let us note \({}_n F\) the kernel of this multiplication; so \({}_n F(X)\), for \(X \in \operatorname{Ob} T\), is the group sections of \(F(X)\) whose order divides \(n\).
We denote by \(\mathbb{P}\) the set of all prime numbers.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1

Let \(p\) be a set of prime numbers. We say that \(F\) is a sheaf of \(p\)-twist, where a \(p\)-sheaf, if the canonical morphism \[ \varinjlim_n {}_n F \longrightarrow F \quad \text{est bijectif} , \] \(n\) traversing the set of integers such as ass \(n \subset p\), that is, tels that the dividing prime numbers \(n\) be in \(p\). If \(p = \mathbb{P}\) is the header seems of all prime numbers, we simply say that \(F\) is torsion.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.2

(i) \(F\) is \(p\)-torsion if and only if \(F\) is the beam associated with a presheaf with values in abelian groups of \(p\)-torsion; we can take \[ P = \varinjlim_{\text{ass } n \subset p}^{(\text{préf})} {}_n F . \]

(ii) If \(F\) is \(p\)-torsion and if \(X \in \operatorname{Ob} T\) is a quasi-compact object(∗), then \(F(X)\) is a \(p\)-twist group.

(iii) If \(T\) is locally of finite type (VI 1.1), then \(F\) is of \(p\)-twist if and only if \(F(X)\) is a group of \(p\)-twist for each \(X \in \operatorname{Ob} T\) quasi-compact. In this case, we have \[ \varinjlim_{\text{ass } n \subset p} H^q(T/X; {}_n F) \xrightarrow{\sim} H^q(T/X; F) \] for every \(X\) quasi-compact and all \(q\).

(iv) If \(u : T \to T'\) is a topos morphism and if \(F'\) is \(p\)-torsion on \(T'\), then the inverse image \(u^* F'\) is a \(p\)-twist beam on \(T\).

(v) If \(u : T \to T'\) is a topos morphism, with \(T\) and \(T'\) locally of finite type and \(u\) quasi-compact(∗), and if \(F\) is a bundle of \(p\)-twist on \(T\), then the \(R^q u_* F'\) are \(p\)-twist bundles on \(T\) for all \(q\).

Proof: (i) Obviously, if a beam \(F\) is of \(p\)-torsion, \(F\) is the beam associated with the pre-beam \(P\) whose group of sections \(P(X)\) is the subgroup of \(p\)-twist elements of \(F(X)\). Conversely, either \(F = \underline{a} P\) (II) the bundle associated with \(P\), where \(P\) has values in the category \(p\)-torsion abelian groups. From the exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow {}_n P \longrightarrow P \xrightarrow{\ n\ } P \] we deduce an exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow \underline{a}({}_n P) \longrightarrow F \xrightarrow{\ n\ } F , \] hence \(\underline{a}({}_n P) = {}_n F\). As the functor \(\underline{a}\) commutes at inductive limits, we deducted from \[ \varinjlim_{\text{ass } n \subset p}^{(\text{préf})} {}_n P \xrightarrow{\sim} P \] that we also have \[ \varinjlim_{\text{ass } n \subset p} {}_n F \xrightarrow{\sim} F . \]

(ii) Let us set \(P = \varinjlim^{(\text{préf})} {}_n F\). Then we have \(P \subset F\), so \(P\) is a pre- separated beam, and consequently \[ \underline{a} P(X) = \varinjlim \left( \ker\left( \textstyle\prod_i P(X_i) \rightrightarrows \right) \right) , \] where as usual the limit is taken according to the covering families \(\{ X_i \longrightarrow X \}\). But as \(X\) is almost compact, you just need to take the finite covering families. For these families \(\prod_i P(X_i)\) is \(p\)-twist; so the \(\ker\) is too. Therefore \(\underline{a} P(X) = F(X)\) is \(p\)-torsion.

(iii) To verify that \(\varinjlim_n {}_n F \longrightarrow F\) is bijective, simply do for values on \(X \in \operatorname{Ob} T\) for each \(X\) of a family of generators. We are therefore brought back to (ii) for the first assertion. The second statement is a consequence of
(iv) Consequence of (i) since \(u^*(\underline{a} P') = \underline{a}(u' P)\)
(v) Easy consequence of (iii).

We will also need a non-abelian variant. But there is technical problems in the definition (I don't know if they are serious), and we will therefore be content to give a definition for the sparse topology of a diagram.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.3

Let \(p\) be a set of prime numbers. It is said that a group \(G\) is an ind-\(p\)-group if each finite subset of \(G\) generates a sub-group finite group of order \(n\), with ass \(n \subset p\). It is the same thing to say that the finite subgroups \(G_\alpha\) of order \(n_\alpha\), with ass \(n_\alpha \subset p\), form a set filtering, and that \(G = \varinjlim G_\alpha\). If \(p = \mathbb{P}\), we say ind-finite group instead from ind-\(p\)-group.
We immediately check the

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.4

(i) A subgroup as well as a quotient of an ind \(p\)-group is still a ind \(p\)-group.

(ii) A filtering inductive limit of ind-\(p\)-groups is an ind-\(p\)-group.

(iii) A finite projective limit of ind-\(p\)-groups is an ind-\(p\)-group.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.5

Let \(X\) be a schema. We say that a sheaf \(F\) of groups on \(X\) is a bundle of ind-\(p\)-groups (resp. of ind-finite groups) if for each \(U \longrightarrow X\) spread, with \(U\) quasi-compact, \(F(U)\) is an ind \(p\)-group (resp. an ind-finite group).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.6

Let \(F\) be a bundle of groups on the diagram \(X\).

(i) \(F\) is a bundle of ind-\(p\)-groups if and only if for each geometric point \(\xi\) of \(X\), fiber \(F_\xi\) is an ind-\(p\)-group.

(ii) If \(f : X \longrightarrow X'\) is a morphism and if \(F'\) is a sheaf of ind-\(p\)-groups on \(X'\), \(f^* F'\) is a bundle of ind-\(p\)-groups.

(iii) If \(f : X \longrightarrow X'\) is a quasi-compact morphism and \(F\) is a sheaf of ind-\(p\)-groups on \(X\), then \(f_* F\) is a bundle of ind-\(p\)-groups.

Demonstration. (i) Suppose that \(F\) is a bundle of ind-\(p\)-groups, and let \(\xi\) be a geometric point of \(X\). Then \(F = \varinjlim F(X')\), where \(X'\) iterates a pseudo-filtering system of patterns spread over \(X\) (VIII 3.9). It is obvious that the \(X'\) affines (therefore quasi-compact) form a cofinal set, and that hence \(F\) is an inductive limit of ind \(p\)-groups, therefore is an ind-\(p\)-group from 1.4 (ii). Conversely, suppose that for each \(\xi\) the fiber \(F_\xi\) either an ind-\(p\)-group and let \(U \longrightarrow X\) be slack, \(U\) quasi-compact. Or \(S \subset F(U)\) a finite subset. For each geometric point \(\xi\) of \(U\), the image of \(S\) in \(F_\xi\) generates a finite group. As a finite group is presentation finished, we easily conclude that there exists a \(U_\xi\) spread on \(U\), \(\xi\)-punctuated, such that \(S\) generates a finite group in \(F(U_\xi)\) (cf. VIII 4). A finite number such \(U_\xi\) form an overlap of \(U\), i.e. \(\{ U_1, \ldots, U_n \}\). So the image of \(S\) in \(\prod F(U_i)\) generates a finite subgroup, and as \(F(U) \subset \prod F(U_i)\), we won.
Assertion (ii) is trivial from (i), and (iii) is immediate.

footnotes.html: (∗) An object \(X\) of a topos is said to be quasi-compact if each covering family \(\{ X_i \longrightarrow X \}\) can be increased by a finite covering family. A morphism \(u\) of topos is quasi-compact if the inverse image of each quasi-compact object is still quasi-compact.

blocks.html: 2. Buildable bundles

Let \(T\) be a topos. Recall that for each element \(S \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathrm{Ens})\) we associates the beam \(S_T\) associated with the pre-beam \(P\) such that \(P(X) = S\) for every \(X \in \operatorname{Ob}(T)\). The object of \(T\) which represents \(S_T\) is \[ \coprod_{s \in S} e = \text{"} S \times e \text{"} , \] \(e\) the final object of \(T\). \(S_T\) is called constant beam, of value \(S\) (IV). We clearly define the notion of a bundle of constant groups, of \(A\)-constant modules (\(A\) a ring), and of constant abelian sheaf, of value \(M\). A morphism \(f_T : S_T \longrightarrow S'_T\) of constant sheaves is called constant if it comes from a set morphism \(f : S \longrightarrow S'\).
A beam \(F\) is said to be locally constant if there is an overlap \(\{ e_i \longrightarrow e \}\) of the final object such that \(F\) becomes constant on each \(e_i\).

We define in an analogous way the notion of bundle of groups or of \(A\)-modules locally constant, and of locally constant morphism \(f : F \to G\) if \(F\) and \(G\) are locally constant sheaves. Finally, a bundle of groups or locally constant \(A\)-modules is said to be of finite type (resp. of presentation finite) if the local values are groups or \(A\)-modules of finite type (resp. finished presentation).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1

(i) Let \(f : F \to G\) a morphism of sheaves of sets locally constant on \(T\), and suppose that the local values of \(F\) are finite sets. Then \(f\) is locally constant.

(ii) Let \(f : F \longrightarrow G\) be a morphism of sheaves of \(A\)-modules locally constants, with \(F\) of finite type. Then \(f\) is locally constant, and the kernel and cokernel of \(f\) are locally constant.

(iii) Let \(0 \to F' \to F \to F'' \to 0\) an exact sequence of sheaves of \(A\)-modules (resp. groups), with \(F'\) and \(F''\) locally constants, \(F''\) being of finished presentation. Then \(F\) is locally constant.

Proof of (i) and (ii). We easily return to the case where \(F\) and \(G\) are constant. Let us process (ii): let \(F = M_T\), \(G = N_T\) (\(M, N\) of \(A\)-modules), and let \(S \subset M\) be a finite subset of generators of \(M\). So \(f\) is determined by its restriction to \(S_T\) and \(f\) will be locally constant if \(f | S_T\) is. We are therefore reduced to (i) for the first assertion of (ii), and the second assertion is a trivial consequence of the first.

It therefore remains to demonstrate (i). Suppose \(F = S_T\), \(G = S'_T\) are constant, and let us also denote by \(f\) the morphism of objects \(f : S \times e \longrightarrow S' \times e\) (notation as above). Let \(f_s : e \longrightarrow S' \times e\) (\(s \in S\)) be the components of \(f\): since \(S\) is finished, it is obviously sufficient to treat the case \(f = f_s\), that is, where \(S\) is a set of one element. Or \(X_{s'} \longrightarrow e\) (\(s' \in S'\)) the family of morphisms which make diagrams Cartesian \[ \xymatrix{ X_{s'} \ar[r] \ar[d] & e \ar[d]^{i_{s'}} \\ e \ar[r]_-{f} & S' \times e } \] where \(i_{s'} : e \longrightarrow S' \times e\) is the "inclusion" in the \(s'\)-th component. The family \(\{ i_{s'} \}\) is trivially covering, so \(\{ X_{s'} \longrightarrow e \}\) is too. We check immediately (since direct sums in a topos are disjoint) that \(f\) becomes constant on \(X_{s'}\), and that consequently \(f\) is locally constant.

Proof of (iii). Let us deal with the case of bundles of \(A\)-modules. We can assume \(F'\) and \(F''\) constants, \(F''\) of value \(M''\) a \(A\)-module of finished presentation. If \(M''\) is free, therefore admits a finite basis \((e_i)_{1 \leqslant i \leqslant n}\), then the \(e_i\) define sections of \(F''\), therefore are raised locally in sections of \(F\), which shows that locally the extension \(F\) of \(F''\) by \(F'\) splits, which proves that \(F\) is locally isomorphic to \(F' \times F''\), so is locally constant. In the general case, choosing a homomorphism over- jective \(\varphi : L'' \to M''\), with free \(L''\) of finite type; the kernel \(R\) of \(\varphi\) is of finite type, and setting \(L = L''_T \times_{M''_T} F\), \(F \simeq L''_T \times_{F''} F\); we see on the one hand that \(L\) is an extension of \(L''_T\) by \(G\), therefore locally constant according to what precedes, on the other hand that we have an exact sequence \(0 \longrightarrow R_T \longrightarrow L \longrightarrow F \longrightarrow 0\), which thanks to (ii) implies that \(F\) is locally constant.
From now on, we limit ourselves to the spread topos of the diagrams.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2

Let \(X\) be a schema and \(F\) be a beam on \(X\) which is local- ment constant. Then \(F\) is represented by a \(Y/X\) slack. If moreover the fibers of \(F\) are finite (resp. and non-empty), then \(Y\) is a coating flat (resp. and surjective).
Demonstration. Descent (SGA 1 IX 4.1 if \(F\) with finite fibers, SGA 3 X 5.4 in the general case).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.3

A bundle of sets (resp. groups, resp. \(A\)-modules) is said to be constructible if for each open affine \(U \subset X\) there exists a decomposition of \(U\) into the union of a finite number of constructible sub-schemas locally closed reduced \(U_i\) such that the beam induced by \(F\) on each \(U_i\) is locally constant, of finite value (resp. finite, resp. of finite presentation(∗)).

The hypothesis of finiteness on local values amounts to saying that for each geometric point \(\xi\), the fiber \(F_\xi\) is finite (resp. finite, resp. of finished presentation). It immediately follows from 2.1 (i) that a bundle of groups \(F\) is constructible if and only if the bundle of sub-sets underlying \(F\) is constructible.

We will pay attention that if \(F\) is an abelian sheaf, it is constructed tible as a bundle of groups (or again, as a bundle of sets) bles) if and only if it is constructible as a bundle of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-modules, and if more its fibers are finished. Thus, \(\mathbb{Z}_X\) is constructible as \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module, but not as a bundle of groups.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.4

(i) Let \(X\) quasi-compact and quasi-separate. So a bundle (resp. group bundle, resp. bundle of \(A\)-modules) is constructed tible if and only if there is a decomposition of \(X\) in a meeting of parties locally closed constructibles, such that \(F\) becomes locally constant, and finite (resp. finite, resp. of finished presentation), on each \(X_i\).

(ii) To verify that \(F\) is constructible, it suffices to verify that \(F | U_i\) is constructible for \(U_i\) of a given open covering of \(X\).

(iii) Let \(f : Y \longrightarrow X\) be a morphism. Then \(f^* F\) is constructible if \(F\) is.

(iv) The set of points where the fiber of a constructible beam is non- empty (resp. non-null) is a locally constructible subset (EGA \(\mathrm{O_{III}}\) 9.1.2).

(v) Let \(X\) locally Noetherian. Then a bundle of sets (resp. ...) \(F\) on \(X\) is constructible if and only if for each \(x \in X\), it exists a non-vacuum open of adhesion \(\bar{x}\) of \(x\) such that \(F\) induces a beam locally constant of finite value (resp. ...) on \(U\).

Demonstration. Obviously, if \(f : Y \longrightarrow X\) is a morphism and if it exists a decomposition of \(X\) into a union of parts \(X_i\) locally closed and constructible, such that \(F\) becomes locally constant on each \(X_i\), it is likewise of \(Y\) and \(f^* F\) (see EGA IV 1.8.2). This proves the implication \(\Longleftarrow\) of (i). Now suppose that \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated and is \(\{ U_1, \ldots, U_n \}\) an affine open covering of \(X\) such that \(F | U_i\) is constructed tible for each \(i\). To find a decomposition of \(X\) as in (i), it just do it for \(U_n\) and for \(Y = X - U_n\) (with the induced structure re- duite), \(U_n\) and \(Y\) being constructible in \(X\) thanks to the hypothesis "\(X\) quasi- separated." But a decomposition exists for \(U_n\) according to the definition of construct- tibility, and \(Y\) is a union of \(n-1\) open affines \(V_i = Y \cap U_i\) (\(i = 1, \ldots, n-1\)) with \(F | V_i\) constructible, hence the result (i) by induction on \(n\). The same reasoning demonstrates (ii); indeed, the hypothesis implies that \(F | U\) is constructible for each open affine \(U\) "small enough", and we can cover an arbitrary affine open \(V\) by a finite number of such open sets. Assertion (ii) shows that the notion of constructibility is local on \(X\), and (iii), (iv) immediately follow. The \(\Longrightarrow\) implication of (v) is immediate, and does not depend on the Noetherian hypothesis. If \(X\) is Noetherian we prove the implication \(\Longleftarrow\) by the usual "Noetherian recurrence".

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.5

Let \(X\) quasi-compact and quasi-separate, and \(F\) a beam groups or \(A\)-modules buildable on \(X\). So there is a filter- finite version of \(F\) whose successive quotients are of the form \(i_! G\), where \(i : U \to X\) is the inclusion of a locally closed and constructible part, and where \(G\) is locally constant and constructible on \(U\). If \(X\) is Noetherian, it exists such filtration, with \(U\) irreducible.

Demonstration. According to 2.4 (i), there exists a decomposition of \(X\) into finite union of locally closed constructible parts \(X_i\) such that each \(F | X_i\) is locally constant and finite (resp. of finite presentation). Let's write \(X_i = U_i \cap \complement V_i\), where \(U_i\) and \(V_i\) are open constructibles, and let \(N\) the number of openings of \(X\) in the subtopology \(T\) generated by \(\{ U_i, V_i \}\). Let us reason by induction on \(N\): if \(W\) is a minimal non-empty element of \(T\), it is obvious that \(F | W\) is locally constant and of finite presentation. Let \(i : W \to X\) be the inclusion morphism and \(Y = X - W\). We have the exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow i_! i^* F \longrightarrow F \longrightarrow F | Y \longrightarrow 0 , \] and we thus reduce ourselves to the same assertion for \(Y\) and for the beam \(F | Y\), where we can apply the recurrence hypothesis.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.6

Suppose that \(A\) is a Noetherian ring.

(i) A finite projective (resp. inductive) limit of sheaves of \(A\)-modules or constructible sets is constructible. In particular, the kernel, the cokernel and the image of a morphism of sheaves of \(A\)-modules buildable are buildable.

(ibis) A finite projective limit of sheaves of sets (resp. groups) constructibles is constructible, and if \(f : F \longrightarrow G\) is a morphism of bundles of constructible groups, the core, the image, and the co-core (if \(\operatorname{Im}(f)\) is a normal subgroup) are constructible. An inductive limit finite bundles of constructible sets are constructible.

(ii) Let \(0 \longrightarrow M' \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow M'' \longrightarrow 0\) an exact sequence of sheaves of \(A\)-modules (resp. groups) with \(M'\), \(M''\) constructibles. So \(M\) is buildable.

(iii) Let \(M_1 \longrightarrow M_2 \longrightarrow M_3 \longrightarrow M_4 \longrightarrow M_5\) an exact sequence of sheaves from \(A\)-modules. So if \(M_i\) is constructible for \(i = 1,2,4,5\), it is of same for \(i = 3\).

Demonstration. (i) We leave the set assertions and for groups to the reader. For the case of bundles of \(A\)-modules, it suffices to de- show that the core and cokernel of a morphism \(f : F \to G\) of sheaves of \(A\)-buildable modules are buildable. The assertion is local to \(X\), and we can therefore assume \(X\) affine, therefore quasi-compact and quasi-separate. So we reduce to the case where \(F\) and \(G\) are locally constant by 2.4. (i), and we ends using 2.1 (ii) and the Noetherian hypothesis on \(A\). We prove analogously assertion (ii) using 2.1 (iii). The assertion (iii) is immediate from (i) and (ii).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.7

Let \(X\) a quasi-compact and quasi-separate scheme, \(F\) a bundle of sets (resp. of \(A\)-modules). So that \(F\) is constructible, it is necessary and sufficient that it is isomorphic to the cokernel of a pair of morphisms \(H \rightrightarrows G\), where \(H\) and \(G\) are sheaves of sets representable by finite presentation flat diagrams on \(X\) (resp. that \(F\) is isomorphic at the core of a homomorphism \(A_{V,X} \longrightarrow A_{U,X}\), where \(U\) and \(V\) are two schemes finished presentation spreads on \(X\)).
Sufficiency easily follows from 2.6 (ibis).

Suppose that \(F\) is a bundle of constructible sets. Using the existence of infinite sums in the site spread over \(X\), we see that we can find an epimorphism \(G \to F\), with \(G\) representable by a schema spreads over \(X\), which we can further assume as sum of affine schemas \(G_i\) (\(i \in I\)), therefore separated on \(S\). For any finite part \(J\) of the information set dices \(I\), i.e. \(G_J\) the sum of \(G_i\) for \(i \in J\), \(F_J\) its image in \(F\). As \(G_J\) and \(F\) are constructible, so is \(F_J\) (2.6 (ibis)), therefore the set \(X_J\) of \(x \in X\) such that the fibers of \(F\) and \(F_J\) at a point geometric of \(X\) on \(x\) are equal, is locally constructible 2.4 (iv). As the family of \(X_J\) is growing and meeting \(X\), and that \(X\) is quasi-compact, one of \(X_J\) is equal to \(X\) (EGA IV 1.9.9). This shows, even if it means replacing \(G\) by \(G_J\), which we can assume is \(G\) affine, therefore separated on \(S\), and quasi-compact on \(X\) since \(X\) is quasi-separated (EGA IV 1.2.4). Let then be \(H = G \times_X G\). It is a sub-bundle of \(G \times_S G\) which is representable, therefore is itself representable (VIII 6.1). As \(G\) is more constructible (2.6 (ibis)), it still follows from the previous argument that it is quasi-compact, therefore (being separated on \(X\)) from finite presentation on \(X\). This proves the first first assertion of 2.7, and the second is proven by the same method (NB it is not necessary that \(A\) be Noetherian). Let us point out in passing that the demonstration also proves the following corollary:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.7.1

Let \(X\) be a schema, \(F\) be a schema spread over \(X\). For the corresponding spread beam on \(X\) to be constructible, it is necessary and it is enough that \(F\) is of finite presentation on \(X\).
Proposition 2.7 will be especially useful here to deduce certain limit crossing properties for constructible beams:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.7.2

Let \(X\) be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme. So any bundle of sets (resp. of ind-\(p\)-groups, resp. of \(A\)-modules) on \(X\) is the inductive limit of an inductive filtering system of bundles of assemblies (resp. ...) constructible.

If \(F\) is a bundle of sets, let us return to the notations of the demonstration of 2.7, and let \(L\) be the set of pairs \((J, H')\), where \(J\) is a finite part of \(I\) and \(H'\) a quasi-compact open part of \(H_J = G_J \times_F G_J\). We order \(L\) in the obvious way, and we note that, since \(F\) is limit of \(F_J\), and that each \(F_J\) is limit of \(\operatorname{Coker}(H' \rightrightarrows G_J)\) for the open ones quasi-compact \(H'\) of \(H_J\), that \(F\) is the inductive limit of the inductive system of \(F_\ell = \operatorname{Coker}(H' \rightrightarrows F_J)\) for \(\ell = (H', J) \in L\), hence the set case. The case bundles of \(A\)-modules are treated in essentially the same way, including starting by representing \(F\) as the cokernel of a homomorphism \(A_{V,X} \longrightarrow A_{U,X}\), with \(U, V\) spread and separated schemas on \(X\). The case of ind-\(p\)-groups requires a little more attention. Taking up the previous notations, we de- let us sign by \(L(G_J)\) the free group beam generated by the incoming beam seems \(G_J\), by \(N_J\) the kernel of the homomorphism \(L(G_J) \to F\) deduced from \(G_J \to F\), by \(L\) the set of pairs \((J, N')\), where \(N'\) is a sub-bundle of sets of \(N_J\) which is "quasi-compact" i.e. such that an epimorphism exists \(P \to N'\), with \(P\) representable by a \(X\)-quasi-compact scheme. We order \(L\) by declaring that \((J, N')\) is smaller than \((J_1, N'_1)\) if \(J \subset J_1\) and if the canonical homomorphism \(L(G_J) \to L(G_{J_1})\) applies \(N'\) in \(N'_1\). He is immediate that \(L\) is filtering, and that we obtain an inductive system of beams in groups \(F_\ell\), indexed by \(L\), taking, for \(\ell = (J, N')\), \(F_\ell = \) beam in groups quotient of \(L(G_J)\) by the generated invariant group sub-sheaf by \(N'\). It is more immediate that \(F\) is isomorphic to the inductive limit of \(F_\ell\), and it remains to be proven that there exists a \(L'\) part of \(L\), cofinal in \(L\), such that for \(\ell \in L'\), \(F_\ell\) is a beam in \(p\)-groups constructible. For this, it suffices to prove that for all \(J\), we can find a sub- bundle of sets \(N'\) of \(N_J\) which is quasi-compact, contains a sub- given quasi-compact bundle of sets \(N'_0\), and be such that the \(F_\ell\) corres- laying either a beam in \(p\)-constructible groups. Let us first note that it is sufficient made, for this, that the fibers of \(F_\ell\) are finite \(p\)-groups: then \(F_\ell\) will be automatically constructible, as the reader will easily verify. On the other hand, the reader will also be able to verify that all \(X_{N'}\) of points \(x\) of \(X\) such that the fiber of \(F_\ell\) at a geometric point above \(x\) or a finite \(p\)-group is locally constructible. In addition, the parties \(X_{N'}\) of \(X\) are an increasing function of \(N'\), finally their meeting is \(X\) all integer: this last point results from the fact that the inductive limit of \(F_\ell\), for \(\ell = (N', J)\) with \(J\) set, is the group sub-bundle \(F_J\) of \(F\) generated by the sub-sheaf of sets \(G_J\), and \(F_J\) is with fibers of \(p\)-finite groups. On the other hand, in a free group with a finite number of generators, any subgroup of finite index is a subgroup free to a finite number of generators, so if it is represented as meeting filtering its finite parts, one of these generates the entire subgroup. From this it easily follows that the meeting of \(X_{N'}\) is indeed \(X\). Using again (EGA IV 1.9.9) we find that one of \(X_{N'}\) is equal to \(X\), which completes the daemons- version of 2.7.2.

Note that the somewhat painful demonstration of the case of diagrams in groups- pes highlights the lack of a convenient outlet for the conditions of cons- tructibility for bundles in groups of fibers not necessarily finite; we apologize to the reader and invite them, if necessary, to fill in this shortcoming by his own means, and let us point out to him by way of consolation that in 2.9 (iii) we obtain a simpler proof when \(X\) is Noetherian.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.7.3

Let \(X\) be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme, \(F\) a bundle of sets (resp. of \(A\)-modules) constructible. Then the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}(F,G)\), where \(G\) is a sheaf of sets (resp. ...) variable, switches to inductive limits in \(G\). In case \(F\) is a do- that of \(A\)-modules, \(A\) being Noetherian, the functors \(\operatorname{Ext}^i(X; F, G)\) in it \(A\)-module \(G\) also commute at inductive limits.
This follows from 2.7 and (VII 3.3) by the usual arguments, which are left to the reader. Likewise, the conjunction of 2.7 and the results of (VII 5) easily gives:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.7.4

Let \(I\) be a filtering category, \(i \mapsto X_i : I^{\circ} \to (\mathrm{Sch})\) be a functor which transforms arrows into affine morphisms and objects into quasi-compact and quasi-separated schemes. For any schema \(Y\), either \(F_c(Y)\) the category of bundles of sets (resp. of ind-\(p\)-groups, resp. of \(A\)-modules) buildable on \(Y\). So we have an equivalence of categories \[ F_c(X) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_i F_c(X_i) , \] where \(X = \varprojlim_i X_i\) (VII 5) and where the second member designates the category \(\varprojlim\) of fiber categories, defined in . In particular, any bundle of sets (resp. ...) constructible on \(X\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of a sheaf of same nature on one of \(X_i\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.8

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a surjective and locally morphism of finite presentation, and let \(F\) a bundle of sets (resp. ...) on \(Y\). Then \(F\) is constructible if and only if \(f^*(F)\) is.
We will use the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.8.1

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) a surjective and locally presenta- morphism finite formation, with \(Y\) quasi-compact and quasi-separated. So there is a partition finite of \(Y\) in subschemas \(Y_i\) of finite presentation on \(Y\) (in particularly each \(Y_i\) is constructible in \(X\)) and for all \(i\), morphisms finite surjectives \(Y''_i \xrightarrow{\ g_i\ } Y'_i \xrightarrow{\ h_i\ } Y_i\), with \(h_i\) spread and \(g_i\) locally free (i.e. flat and finished presentation, in addition to the condition \(g_i\) finished), and root, and finally a \(Y\)-morphism \(Y''_i \longrightarrow X\)(∗).

Using the fact that \(Y\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated, we get easily in the case where \(Y\) is quasi-affine and therefore separated (using an overlap open finite affine \((U_i)_{1 \leqslant i \leqslant n}\) of \(Y\) and considering the \(Y_i = U_i - U_i \cap \bigcup_{j < i} U_j\), then in the case \(Y\) affines (by the same argument). The schema \(X\) is a meeting of affine greens \(X_i\), and the image \(T_i\) of \(X_i\) in \(Y\) is constructible (EGA IV 1.8.4), the union of \(T_i\) is \(Y\) since \(f\) is surjective, from which it follows that \(X\) is the union of a finite number of \(T_i\) (EGA IV 1.9.9), so that, rem- placing \(X\) by the sum schema of the corresponding \(X_i\), we can assume \(X\) affines. Then the process of passing to the standard limit (EGA IV 8 8.1.2 c)) allows us to suppose further \(Y\) Noetherian. The usual Noetherian recurrence, and the process of passing the limit (EGA IV 8.1.2 a)) brings us back to the case where \(Y\) is reduced to the spectrum of a body \(k\). As then \(X \neq \emptyset\), there exists a closed point \(x \in X\), which corresponds to a finite extension \(k'\) of \(k\), itself an extension root of a separable extension \(k'_s\) of \(k\). We will then take \(Y' = \operatorname{Spec}(k'_s)\), \(Y'' = \operatorname{Spec}(k')\), which completes the demonstration of 2.8.1.

The necessity in 2.8 being trivial (2.4 (iii)), let us prove the sufficiency session. We are then brought back, by the previous lemma, to the case where \(f\) is of the form \(g h\), where \(g\) and \(h\) satisfy the conditions stated for \(g_i, h_i\) in the lemma. We can therefore suppose successively that \(f\) is finitely radicial, a trivial case by (VIII 1.1), or that \(f\) is finitely equated. Note also that, using the hypothesis that \(f^*(F)\) is constructible and the definition of constructability, we could assume (even if it meant replacing \(X\) by a \(X\)-preschema \(X' \simeq \coprod_i X_i\) of finite presentation and with surjective structural morphism) that \(f^*(F)\) is already local ment constant. But when \(f\) is equal and surjective, this implies that \(F\) is locally constant. As moreover all the hypotheses made on the fibers of \(f^*(F)\) remain valid for those of \(F\), \(f\) being surjective, it follows that \(F\) is constructible, which completes the proof of 2.8.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.9

Let \(X\) a Noetherian scheme, \(A\) a Noetherian ring, and \(p\) a set of prime numbers.

(i) The category of bundles of sets (resp. of ind \(p\)-groups, resp. of \(A\)-modules) on \(X\) is locally Noetherian, that is to say, has a set of generators formed from Noetherian objects.

(ii) A bundle \(F\) sets (resp. of ind \(p\)-groups, resp. of \(A\)-modules) on \(X\) is constructible if and only if it is Noetherian. If \(F\) is a bundle of \(A\)-modules, then \(F\) is constructible if and only- ment if it is the quotient of a finite sum of sheaves of the form \(A_{U/X}\), where \(U \longrightarrow X\) is slack and of finite type (notation of IV.2.4: \(A_{U/X} = A_U\)).

(iii) Constructible sub-sheaves of a beam \(F\) sets (resp. ...) form an inductive system, and \(F\) is the inductive limit of these sub-beams.

Demonstration. We leave the set assertion to the reader. Let's deal first the case of a bundle of \(A\)-modules. For (i) we must find a set ble of generators whose elements are Noetherian objects. Gold the beams of the form \(A_{U/X}\), \(U \longrightarrow X\) and of finite type form a set of general tors which we can easily see that they are constructible, and it is enough therefore to prove the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.10

Let \(X\) noetherian. Then every \(A\)-Buildable module is Noetherian.
Proof of the lemma.

By Noetherian induction we assume the lemma is true for each subschema \(X'\) of \(X\) distinct from \(X\). Let's take a non-empty open \(X_0\) of \(X\) such that \(F\) induces a locally constant beam \(F_0\) on \(X_0\).

Let now \(\{ F_i \}\), \(i \in \mathbb{N}\) be an increasing sequence of sub-sheaves from \(F_0\). As for a geometric point \(P\), generic for an irre- ducible of \(X_0\), the fiber of \(F_0\) in \(P\) is of finite type, the sequence of fibers \((F_i)_P\) is stationary, and we can assume that it is constant. But be it \(Q\) a geometric point of \(X_0\) specialization of \(P\) (VIII 7.2). Since \(F_0\) is locally constant on \(X_0\), we immediately see that the specialist morphism sation \(F_{0_Q} \longrightarrow F_{0_P}\) (VIII.7) is bijective, therefore \((F_i)_Q \longrightarrow (F_i)_P\) is injective for each \(i\).

Let \(V/X\) be flat and \(P\)-punctuated, and \(s_1, \ldots, s_n\) be sections \(\in F_1(V)\) which generate \((F_i)_P\). Then the \(s_i\) generate \((F_i)_Q\) for each \(i\) and each geometric point \(Q\) specialization of \(P\) above \(V\). The sequel of beams \(F_i\) is therefore constant in a neighborhood of the image of \(P\), say in \(X' \neq \emptyset\). Let \(Y = X - X'\), it is therefore enough to prove that the sequence of \(F_i | Y\) is stationary, which results from the Noetherian recurrence hypothesis.

Assertions (ii) for \(A\)-modules are now immediate: As the \(A_{U/X}\) are Noetherian generators, we obviously have \(F\) Noetherian \(\Longleftrightarrow F\) is the quotient of a finite sum of sheaves \(A_{U/X}\). But \(A_{U/X}\) is constructible, and consequently, taking into account 2.6, \(F\) Noetherian \(\Longrightarrow F\) constructible; the impli- inverse cation being already established (2.10), this establishes (ii). Finally, the assertion (iii) for \(A\)-modules is a property of locally Noetherian categories (cf. [1], Ch. IV).

Let's now deal with the case of ind-\(p\)-groups. Let us first note that a constructible bundle of groups \(F\) is certainly Noetherian. Indeed, since the fibers are finite, we can use the same argument than with \(A\)-modules. We will now prove assertion (iii) directly, which will imply (i) - indeed, this will demonstrate that the constructible beams form a set of generators. Moreover, any Noetherian beam will then necessarily be constructible, hence (ii).

To demonstrate (iii), it is obviously sufficient to demonstrate that a sub- bundle (in groups) \(S\) of a bundle \(F\) of ind \(p\)-groups generated by a number finite sections \(s_i \in F(U_i)\), \(U_i \longrightarrow X\) spread of finite type, is constructible(∗).

(Since a constructible sheaf is Noetherian, it is generated by a number finite sections.) By Noetherian induction, we can assume that it is true for each closed subschema \(Y\) of \(X\), distinct from \(X\). However, the notion of sub- beam generated by sections commutes with the inverse image of beams; in effect, let \(L_{U/X}\) be the sheaf which represents the functor \(F \longmapsto F(U)\) in the category of groups. So if \(f : Y \longrightarrow X\), we have \(f^*(L_{U/X}) = L_{U \times_X Y/Y}\). Like \(S\) is the image of the sum of \(L_{U_i/X}\), and as \(f^*\) commutes to the sums, it is clear that \(f^* S\) is the sub-bundle of \(f^* F\) generated by the sections \(f^*(S_i)\).

We can therefore assume that \(S\) induces a constructible beam on each closed \(Y\) distinct from \(X\), and it is thus sufficient (2.4 (i)) to demonstrate that \(S\) induces a constructible beam on a suitable non-empty open of \(X\). By replacing \(X\) with a fairly small open, we can assume that each \(U_i\) is a flat coating of \(X\). It suffices to demonstrate that then \(S\) is locally constant, with finite fibers on a suitable open non-vacuum. This is an assertion local on \(X\) for the sparse topology, and we can therefore assume each \(U_i\) is completely decomposed, say \(U_i = \coprod_{n_i} X\) (sum of \(n_i\) copies of \(X\)). Let \(s_{ij}\) (\(j = 1, \ldots, n_i\)) be the sections of \(F(X)\) such that \(s_i\) \((s_{i1}, \ldots, s_{in_i})\). Obviously, \(S\) is none other than the sub-beam of \(F\) generated by the \(s_{ij} \in F(X)\). As \(F(X)\) is an ind \(p\)-group (1.5) the \(s_{ij}\) generates a finite subgroup 1.3. So we can assume that the set of \(s_i\) is a sub- finite group of \(F(X)\), but then \(S\) is identical to the generated set sheaf by \(s_i\), which is constructible under 2.6 (i) as a bundle of sets, therefore as a bundle of groups, cqfd.
In the following two propositions, we give criteria for that a beam is locally constant, or constructible, using the homo- specialization morphisms (VIII 7.7).

Proposal 2.11

Let \(X\) a schema, \(F\) a bundle of sets (resp. of ind-finite groups, resp. of of \(A\)-modules) constructible; in the case assembly, we further assume \(F\) with finite fibers. Let \(x \in X\), \(\bar{x}\) a point geometric on \(x\). So that \(F\) is locally constant in the neighborhood of \(x\), it is necessary and sufficient that for any geometric point \(\bar{x}'\) generation of \(\bar{x}\), and any specialization arrow \(\bar{x}' \to \bar{x}\) (VIII 7.2), the homomorphism of specia- lization (VIII 7.7) \(F_{\bar{x}} \to F_{\bar{x}'}\), or an isomorphism.

The case of a bundle in groups being a particular case of that of a bundle of sets, we are content to treat this one; the case of a that of modules is treated in an essentially identical manner. The need for condition being trivial, we limit ourselves to establishing sufficiency. Like fiber \(I = F_{\bar{x}}\) is finished, even if it means replacing \(X\) with a schema \(X'\) spread over \(X\) suitable for nable, we can assume that we can find a homomorphism \(u : I_X \longrightarrow F\) of the constant beam \(I_X\) in \(F\), inducing an isomorphism for the fibers in \(\bar{x}\). Using the constructibility of the two sheaves, and 2.6, we easily see that the set \(Z\) of points \(z\) of \(X\) such that the homomorphism induced on the fibers at a geometric point \(\bar{z}\) on \(z\) is bijective, is a part locally constructible from \(X\). The hypothesis immediately implies that it contains the genes rizations of \(x\), therefore (EGA IV 1.10.1) it is a neighborhood of \(x\), which proves that \(F\) is locally constant (in this case, even constant) in the neighborhood of \(x\), cqfd.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.12(∗)

A schema \(X\) is said to be connected by arcs if for all pair \(P, Q\) geometric points of \(X\), there exist geometric points \(P = P_0, \ldots, P_n\); \(Q_1, \ldots, Q_n = Q\) and specializations (VIII 7.2) \(P_i \longrightarrow Q_i\) and \(P_{i-1} \longrightarrow Q_i\) (\(i = 1, \ldots, n\)). We say that \(X\) is locally connected by arcs (for the flat topology) if for each \(U \longrightarrow X\) straight there exists an overlap equal \(\{ U_i \longrightarrow U \}\) of \(U\) such that \(U_i\) is connected by arcs for each \(i\).
We immediately verify that a locally Noetherian preschema is locally connected by arcs.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.13

(i) Let \(X\) locally connected by arcs and let \(F\) a sheaf of sets (resp. of groups, resp. of of \(A\)-modules) on \(X\). Assume that the fibers of \(F\) are finished (resp. finite, resp. of finished presentation). So \(F\) is locally constant if and only if for any specialization \(P \longrightarrow Q\) of geometric points, the specialization morphism (VIII 7.7) \(F_Q \longrightarrow F_P\) is bijective.

(ii) Suppose that \(X\) is locally Noetherian and that the fibers of \(F\) are finished (resp. finite, resp. of finished presentation). So, if for all specialization \(P \longrightarrow Q\), the specialization morphism \(F_Q \longrightarrow F_P\) is injected- tif, \(F\) is constructible.

(iii) Suppose that \(X\) is locally Noetherian and that the fibers of bunch of sets \(F\) are finished. Let \(c : X \longrightarrow \mathbb{N}\) the function which \(x \in X\) associates the number of fiber elements \(F_{\bar{x}}\) of \(F\) in a geometric point cudgel \(\bar{x}\) above \(x\). Then \(F\) is constructible if and only if \(c\) is a constructible function, i.e., if and only if \(c^{-1}(n)\) is construct- tible for each \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).

Demonstration. (i) Let \(P\) be a geometric point of \(X\). We will find a slack neighborhood of \(P\), that is to say, a slack \(U \longrightarrow X\) \(P\)-punctuated, such that \(F\) becomes constant on \(U\). Let \(V'\) be a slack neighborhood of \(P\) such that \(F(V) \longrightarrow F_P\) is surjective. Such a \(V'\) exists according to the hypothesis of finitude. Let \(U \longrightarrow V'\) be a slack neighborhood of \(P\) in \(V'\), such that \(U\) is connected by arcs. So \(F(U) \longrightarrow F_P\) is still surjective. Let's choose a subset \(S \subset F(U)\) which is sent bijectively to \(F_P\). If \(F\) is a bundle of groups (resp. of \(A\)-modules), we can easily see that we can, by changing giant if necessary \(U\), find such a set which is also a subgroup (resp. submodule) of \(F(U)\). We deduce a morphism \(S_U \longrightarrow F | U\), where \(S_U\) is the constant beam with value \(S\). We have \[ (S_U)_P \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } F_P . \]
Since each specialization morphism on the fibers of \(F\) (resp. \(S_U\)) is bijective, and since \(U\) is connected by arc, it follows that \(S_U \xrightarrow{\sim} F | U\), hence the result.

(ii) The assertion is local, and we can therefore assume \(X\) Noetherian. Let \(P\) be a geometric point above a maximum point of \(X\). There exists an irreducible slack neighborhood, therefore connected by arcs, \(U \longrightarrow X\) of \(P\) such that \(F(U)\) is sent surjectively to \(F_P\). It is then sent surjectively on \(F_Q\) for each geometric point \(Q\) of \(V\), or what amounts to the same thing, for each geometric point of the image \(U\) of \(V\) in \(X\), because such a point is specialization of \(P\) and \(F_Q \longrightarrow F_P\) is injective. So each morphism of specialization in \(U\) is bijective, so \(F | U\) is locally constant. By Noetherian recurrence we can assume that \(F | X - U\) is constructible, hence the result.

(iii) It is obviously sufficient to deal with the case where the function \(c\) is cons- aunt. Let \(V \longrightarrow X\) be a slack neighborhood of a geometric point \(P\) above of a maximum point of \(X\), such that \(F(V)\) is sent surjectively to \(F_P\). Let \(S\) be a subset of \(F(V)\) such that \(S \xrightarrow{\sim} F_P\). So we have, for the constant beam \(S_V\) and for each specialization \(P \longrightarrow Q\) of geometric points triques, a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ (S_V)_P \ar[r]^-{\sim} & F_P \\ (S_V)_Q \ar[u] \ar[r] & F_Q \ar[u] } \] Like \(c(F_Q) = c(F_P)\), the arrow \(F_Q \longrightarrow F_P\) is bijective, so \(F\) is local- ment constant in a neighborhood of \(P\) according to (i). By recurrence noethé- nothing, we won.
The following result (ii) will have technical utility later; it will reduce certain checks in the case of a constant beam.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.14

Let \(X\) be a schema.

(i) For any finite morphism of finite presentation \(f : X' \to X\), and any bundle of sets (resp. of ind-finite groups, resp. of \(A\)-modules) buildable on \(X'\), \(f_*(F)\) is buildable.

(ii) Suppose \(X\) quasi-compact and quasi-separate, and let \(F\) a beam sets (resp. ...) constructible on \(X\). So we can find a family finite \((p_i : X'_i \longrightarrow X)\) of finite morphisms, for all \(i\), a bundle of en- seems (resp. ...) constructible constant \(C_i\) on \(X'_i\), and a monomorphism \[ F \hookrightarrow \prod_i p_{i*}(C_i) . \]

Let's first prove (i). By definition, we can assume \(X\) affine, and then using 2.7.4, the process of passing to the standard limit gives us brings us back to the case where \(X\) is Noetherian. A new passage to the limit, using again 2.7.4, and further 2.4. (v), brings us back to the case where \(X\) is the spec- be of a body. Then the conclusion is reduced to the corresponding assertion on the fiber of \(f_*(F)\) at a geometric point on \(X\), which results immediately- ment of (VIII 5.5, 5.8).

Let us prove (ii). Under 2.4(i), there is a partition of \(X\) in subschemas \(Y_i\), with inclusion morphisms \(u_i : Y_i \longrightarrow X\) of finite presentation, such that the restriction \(F_i\) of \(F\) to each \(Y_i\) is a locally constant beam. It is then immediate that the cano-homomorphisms niques \(F \longrightarrow u_{i*}(F_i)\) define a homomorphism \(F \longrightarrow \prod_i u_{i*}(F_i)\) which is a monomorphism.

We could even arrange in the previous construction so that each \(F_i\) is, not only locally constant, but also isotrivial, i.e. that there exists a finite surjective morphism \(q_i : Y'_i \longrightarrow Y_i\) such that \(q_i^*(F_i)\) either a constant beam on \(Y'_i\), or \(C_{i\,Y'_i}\). This results by easily example of the definition of 2.8.1 and (VIII 1.1). On the other hand, it follows from the "Main Theorem" in the form (EGA IV 8.12.6) that there exists a finite morphism \(p_i : Z_i \longrightarrow X\) and an open immersion \(v_i : Y'_i \longrightarrow Z_i\) of \(X\)-schemas.

As \(F_i\) immerses itself in \(q_{i*}(q_i^*(F_i)) = q_{i*}(C_{i\,Y'_i})\), \(F\) immerses itself in the product of \(u_{i*}(q_{i*}(C_{i\,Y'_i})) = p_{i*}(v_{i*}(C_{i\,Y'_i}))\). If all \(Z_i\) is normal, then the lemma \((2.14.1)\) below implies that \(v_{i*}(C_{i\,Y'_i}) \xrightarrow{\sim} C_{i\,Z_i}\), so \(F\) dives in the product of \(p_{i*}(C_{i\,Z_i})\), and we're done. In the general case, intro- we derive the normalized \(\overline{Z}_i\) from \(Z_i\), the inverse image \(\overline{Y}'_i\) from \(Y'_i\) into \(\overline{Z}_i\), immersion \(\overline{v}_i : \overline{Y}'_i \longrightarrow \overline{Z}_i\). We immediately see that \(\overline{Y}'_i\) contains the points maximums of \(\overline{Z}_i\), so is dense in \(\overline{Z}_i\). If \(r_i : \overline{Z}_i \to Z_i\) and \(s_i : \overline{Y}'_i \to Y'_i\) are the projections, as the latter is surjective, it follows that \(C_{i\,Y'_i}\) dives into \(s_{i*}(C_{i\,\overline{Y}'_i})\), so \(v_{i*}(C_{i\,Y'_i})\) dives into \(v_{i*}(s_{i*}(C_{i\,\overline{Y}'_i})) = r_{i*}(\overline{v}_{i*}(C_{i\,\overline{Y}'_i}))\), which is isomorphic to \(r_{i*}(C_{i\,\overline{Z}_i})\) under from \((2.14.1)\) below. So \(F\) dives into the product of \(p_{i*}(r_{i*}(C_{i\,\overline{Z}_i})) = t_{i*}(C_{i\,\overline{Z}_i})\), where \(t_i = p_i r_i\) is the canonical projection \(\overline{Z}_i \to X\). When this morphism is finished for all \(i\), we are finished. In the case general, we can only say that \(\overline{Z}_i\) is integer on \(X\), therefore of the form \(\operatorname{Spec}(A_i)\), where \(A_i\) is a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-Algebras, which is integer on \(\mathcal{O}_X\). Using (EGA IV 1.7.7), we see that \(A_i\) is limit induc- tive of its finite sub-Algebras \(A_{i,j}\) on \(X\), so \(\overline{Z}_i\) is projective limit (VII 5) preschemas \(Z_{i\,j} = \operatorname{Spec}(A_{ij})\), finished on \(X\). Applicant (VII 5.11), we find that \(t_{i*}(C_{i\,\overline{Z}_i})\) is the inductive limit of \(t_{ij*}(C_{i\,Z_{ij}})\) where \(t_{ij} : Z_{ij} \to X\) is the canonical projection. By virtue of (2.7.3), homomor- phism \(F \longrightarrow t_{i*}(C_{i\,\overline{Z}_i})\) is therefore factorized by a homomorphism \(F \longrightarrow t_{ij*}(C_{i\,Z_{ij}})\), for a suitable \(j = j(i)\). We now pose \(X'_i = Z_{i,j(i)}\), and we find an embedding as announced in 2.14.
It remains to be proven

Lemma 2.14.1

Let \(X\) a geometrically unibranched diagram (for example normal), \(i : U \longrightarrow X\) quasi-compact open immersion. blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 2.14.2

When \(X\) is Noetherian, the given demonstration (or a immediate deduction) shows that in 2.14 (ii), one can assume the \(X'_i\) gres; if we further assume \(X\) universally Japanese, i.e. the rings of its universally Japanese open affines (EGA \(\mathrm{O_{IV}}\) 23.1.1, IV 7.7.2), we can further assume the normal \(X'_i\).

footnotes.html: (∗) When \(A\) is not assumed to be Noetherian, it is preferable, for the purposes of Homological Algebra, to require here, instead of the only finite presentation for the \(A\)-module \(M\), that it has a left resolution by free \(A\)-modules of finished type. The notion introduced in (2.3) should then take the name: \(F\) is 1-constructible, (more generally, we would obviously define \(n\)-constructibility, for any integer \(n \geqslant 0\)). For the present presentation we will provisionally keep the terminology in the form (2.3), which is especially reasonable in the case where \(A\) is Noetherian, where it coincides with that which we have just pointed out. Compare SGA 6 I for notions of finiteness in non-Noetherian cases.
(∗) This statement is also given in EGA IV 17.16.4.

(∗) Let us recall the definition of this sheaf: let \(L_{U/X}\) be the sheaf which represents the functor \(F \mapsto F(U)\) in the category of groups. The section \(s_i \in F(U_i)\) induces a morphism \(L_{U_i/X} \to F\), and \(S\) is the image of the sum morphism \(\coprod_i L_{U_i/X} \to F\). This is the smallest sub-bundle that "contains" the \(s_i\) sections.
(∗) The terminology is due to S. Lubkin.

blocks.html: 3. Kummer and Artin-Schreier theories

We will note \((\mathbb{G}_m)_X\) the beam represented by the group multiplied cative \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_X[t, t^{-1}]\) on \(X\). So for \(U \longrightarrow X\) spread, the sections of \((\mathbb{G}_m)_X\) of \(U\) are the invertible elements \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}^*_U)\) of \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_U)\). Or \(n \in \mathbb{N}\). The core of the \(n\)-th power in \((\mathbb{G}_m)_X\) is the "bundle of the \(n\)-th roots of the unit", denoted \((\mu_n)_X\). We therefore have a exact sequence \[ (3.1) \qquad 0 \longrightarrow (\mu_n)_X \longrightarrow (\mathbb{G}_m)_X \xrightarrow{\ n\ } (\mathbb{G}_m)_X . \]
If \(n\) is invertible on \(X\) (that is, if \(n\) is prime at each charac- residual teristic of \(X\)), the \(n\)-th power is a surjective morphism for beams:

blocks.title: Kummer theory
blocks.html:
Kummer theory 3.2

We have the exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow (\mu_n)_X \longrightarrow (\mathbb{G}_m)_X \xrightarrow{\ n\ } (\mathbb{G}_m)_X \longrightarrow 0 \] if \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) is invertible on \(X\).

Indeed, let \(u \in (\mathbb{G}_m)_X(U)\), \(U \longrightarrow X\) be equal. We must find an even coverage \(\{ U_i \longrightarrow U \}\) of \(U\) such that \(u\) induces a \(n\)-th power on each \(U_i\). Since \(n\) is invertible on \(U\), the equation \[ T^n - u = 0 \] is "separable" on \(U\), i.e., \[ U' = \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_U[T]/(T^n - u) \] is spread out above \(U\). As \(U' \longrightarrow U\) is surjective, it is covering and \(u\) induces a \(n\)-th power on \(U'\), we are finished.

Let us point out that when \(n\) is invertible on \(X\), \((\mu_n)_X\) is a locally constant beam, in fact locally isomorphic to the beam \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})_X\), and is constant in important cases. Kummer's theory therefore gives information on the cohomology of \(X\) with values in certain some constant beams. In fact, we have by definition \[ H^0(X, (\mathbb{G}_m)_X) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}^*_X) , \] and for the dimension \(1\) we have the

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.3

("Hilbert's theorem 90"): We have an isomorphism \[ H^1(X, (\mathbb{G}_m)_X) \xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Pic} X , \] where \(\operatorname{Pic} X\) is the group of invertible beam classes on \(X\).

Demonstration. This means that the canonical morphism \[ H^1(X_{\text{zar}}, (\mathbb{G}_m)_X) \longrightarrow H^1(X_{\text{ét}}, (\mathbb{G}_m)_X) \] is bijective, which is the same as saying that \[ R^1 \varepsilon_*(\mathbb{G}_m)_X = 0 \] where \(\varepsilon_*\) is the direct image for the obvious site morphism \(\varepsilon : X_{\text{ét}} \longrightarrow X_{\text{zar}}\) (VIII 4). We are therefore reduced to the case where \(X\) is affine. As we can calculate \(H^1\) by Čech (V 2.5) and as it is sufficient for a \(X\) quasi-compact to take quasi-compact coverings, it is a consequence immediate quence of the theory of descent for the beams (cf. FGA 190, or SGA VIII 1).

Let us suppose \(X\) Noetherian and without immersed component and be \(x_j\) the maximum points of \(X\). Let \(R_j\) be the local Artinian ring of \(X\) to \(x_j\), and \(i_j : \operatorname{Spec} R_j \longrightarrow X\) the inclusion morphism.

We have a canonical injection \[ (\mathbb{G}_m)_X \longrightarrow \prod_j i_{j*}(\mathbb{G}_m)_{\operatorname{Spec} R_j} \] of beams on \(X_{\text{ét}}\) (resp. \(X_{\text{zar}}\)). The cokernel \(D\) is called the bundle Cartier divisors on \(X_{\text{ét}}\) (resp. \(X_{\text{zar}}\))(∗).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4

Let \(X\) noetherian and without immersed component, or \(\varepsilon : X_{\text{ét}} \longrightarrow X_{\text{zar}}\) the canonical site morphism, and let \(D\) the bundle Cartier dividers on \(X_{\text{ét}}\). Then \(\varepsilon_* D\) is the beam \(D_{\text{zar}}\) of Cartier divisors on \(X_{\text{zar}}\), and we have in particular: \[ H^0(X_{\text{ét}}, D) \approx H^0(X_{\text{zar}}, D_{\text{zar}}) . \]

Demonstration. If we apply \(\varepsilon_*\) to the exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow (\mathbb{G}_m)_X \longrightarrow \prod_j i_{j*}(\mathbb{G}_m)_{\operatorname{Spec} R_j} \longrightarrow D \longrightarrow 0 , \] we find an exact sequence since \(R^1 \varepsilon_*(\mathbb{G}_m)_X = 0\) (3.3), hence the first assertion.
In the case where \(X\) admits a characteristic \(p \neq 0\), the result following will sometimes replace 3.2 for the study of the coefficients of \(p\)-torsion:

blocks.title: Artin-Schreier theory
blocks.html:
Artin-Schreier theory 3.5

We have the exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})_X \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_X \xrightarrow{\ \wp\ } \mathcal{O}_X \longrightarrow 0 , \] if \(X\) has characteristic \(p > 0\), where \(\wp\) is the morphism of groups additives defined by \(\wp(f) = f^p - f\).

The core of \(\wp\) is the bundle of sections of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) which are locally in the prime body, therefore is a constant beam. Morphism \(\wp\) is surjective for the sparse topology because the equation \(T^p - T - a\) (\(a \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_U)\)) is always "separable" into characteristic \(p\), i.e. defines a flat coating of \(U\). To apply this exact sequence we will use of course (VII 4.2).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.6

(i) Let \(X\) be a schema and \(i : x \longrightarrow X\) the inclusion of a point. So \[ H^1(X, i_*(\mathbb{Z})_x) = 0 \] where \((\mathbb{Z})_x\) is the "constant beam" (see 2.0) of value \(\mathbb{Z}\).

(ii) If \(X\) is irreducible and if for each geometric point \(\bar{x}\) of \(X\) strict localization \(X_{\bar{x}}\) of \(X\) in \(\bar{x}\) is irreducible (we should say "\(X\) is geometrically unibranched" cf. EGA IV 18.8.14) then we have \[ H^1(X, \mathbb{Z}_X) = 0 . \]

Demonstration. (i) From the Leray spectral sequence \[ E_2^{pq} = H^p(X, R^q i_*(\mathbb{Z})_x) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(x, (\mathbb{Z})_x) \] we deduce an injection \(H^1(X, i_*(\mathbb{Z})_x) \longrightarrow H^1(x, (\mathbb{Z})_x)\). Now since \(x = \operatorname{Spec} k(x)\) is the spectrum of a body, it is well known that \(H^1(x, (\mathbb{Z})_x) = 0\) (this results from the fact that \(H^q(x, F)\) (\(q > 0\)) is torsion in in any case (cf. VII 2.2 and CG), and exact consequences \(0 \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z} \xrightarrow{\ n\ } \mathbb{Z} \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}/n \longrightarrow 0\)), hence the result.

(ii) Let \(i : x \to X\) be the inclusion of the generic point of \(X\). We see immediately that under the conditions of (ii) the canonical morphism \((\mathbb{Z})_X \longrightarrow i_*(\mathbb{Z})_x\) is bijective (the converse being true also!), and (ii) is thus reduced to (i).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 3.7

In 3.6 (i) and (ii) we can replace \(\mathbb{Z}\) by any which abelian group without torsion. On the other hand, (ii) becomes false if we abandons the hypothesis that \(X\) is geometrically unibranched, as we see for example by taking for \(X\) an algebraic curve on a field \(K\), admitting an ordinary double point (do the calculation of \(H^1(X, \mathbb{Z})\) in this case!)

footnotes.html: (∗) In EGA IV 21, we simply say "divisors" instead of "Cartier divisors".

blocks.html: 4. Case of an algebraic curve

Let \(X\) be a Noetherian scheme of dimension \(1\). Let \(R(X)\) the ring of rational functions on \(X\), and \(i : \operatorname{Spec} R(X) \longrightarrow X\) the inclusion morphism. The ring \(R(X)\) is artinian, produces rings locals of \(X\) at the maximum points, and we can use the results of (VIII 2).

Let \(F\) be an abelian sheaf on \(\operatorname{Spec} R(X)\) and consider the beams \(R^q i_* F\), \(q > 0\), to \(X\). It immediately follows from VIII 5.2 that the fiber of \(R^q i_* F\) at a geometric point above a maximum point of \(X\) is zero. Since \(X\) is of dimension \(1\), such a beam is of very special nature:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.1

Let \(X\) noetherian and \(F\) a beam on \(X\). The following conditions are equivalent (we will call a beam satisfying under these conditions a "skyscraper sheaf"):

(i) The fiber of \(F\), at any geometric point \(\bar{x}\) of \(X\) which is above a non-closed point \(x \in X\), is zero.

(ii) Each section \(s \in F(X')\) (\(X' \longrightarrow X\) finite type spread) is null, except at a finite number of closed points of \(X\).

(iii) We have \(F \approx \bigoplus_x i_{x*} i_x^* F\), where \(x\) runs through all the points closed from \(X\) and where \(i_x : x \to X\) is the inclusion.

Demonstration. Suppose (i) is true, and either \(z \in F(X')\) (\(X' \longrightarrow X\) finite type spread). So support for \(z\) (VIII 6.6) is a closed part of \(X'\) formed of closed points, therefore finite (\(X'\) being noetherian), hence (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii). The implication (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i) follows from calculation of fibers (VIII 3.9), and (iii) implies (i), because for any family \((G_x)\) of beams on the closed points \(x\) of \(X\), the beam \(F = \bigoplus_x i_{x*}(G_x)\) on \(X\) satisfies (i), as follows from the fact that the fiber functors switch to direct sums, and the support of \(i_{x*}(G_x)\) is obviously contained in \(\{ x \}\).

It remains to demonstrate that (ii) implies (iii). Let's consider the canonical morphism \[ F \longrightarrow \prod_x i_{x*} i_x^* F , \] \(x\) traversing the set of closed points of \(X\). According to (ii), it is factor by \[ F \longrightarrow \bigoplus_x i_{x*} i_x^* F , \] which gives the desired morphism. We leave it to the reader to verify. because it is bijective.

Let's apply the lemma to the sheaf \(R^q i_* F\) introduced above: We have \[ R^q i_* F \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \bigoplus_x i_{x*} i_x^*(R^q i_* F) \] (\(x\) traversing the set of closed points of \(X\)). But according to (VIII 5) the fiber of \(R^q i_* F\) at a geometric point \(\bar{x}\) of \(X\) is \[ (R^q i_* F)_{\bar{x}} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^q(\operatorname{Spec} R(X_{\bar{x}}), F) \] where \(R(X_{\bar{x}}) \xrightarrow{\sim} R(X) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_{X_{\bar{x}}}\), \(X_{\bar{x}}\) being the strict localization of \(X\) in \(\bar{x}\) (VIII 4), and where we also denote by \(F\) the beam induced by \(F\) on \(\operatorname{Spec} R(X_{\bar{x}})\). Now suppose that the residual body of any closed point \(x\) of \(X\) is separably closed. We will therefore have (see also (VI. 1)) \[ \begin{aligned} H^p(X, R^q i_* F) &\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^p\Big(X, \bigoplus_x i_{x*} i_x^*(R^q i_* F)\Big) \\ &\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \bigoplus_x H^p(X, i_{x*} i_x^*(R^q i_* F)) \\ &\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \bigoplus_x H^p(x, i_x^*(R^q i_* F)) \\ &\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \begin{cases} \bigoplus_x H^q(\operatorname{Spec} R(X_x), F) & \text{si } p = 0 \\ 0 & \text{si } p > 0 . \end{cases} \end{aligned} \]
We find the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.2

Let \(X\) a Noetherian schema of dimension \(1\), tel that for any closed point \(x\) of \(X\), the residual body \(k(x)\) is separated- bly closed. Let \(F\) an abelian beam, on \(\operatorname{Spec} R(X)\) (where \(R(X) =\) ring rational functions on \(X\)). Consider the spectral sequence of Leray ( ). \[ E_2^{pq} = H^p(X, R^q i_* F) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\operatorname{Spec} R(X), F) . \] We have \[ E_2^{pq} = 0 \qquad \text{si } p \text{ et } q > 0 , \] \[ E_2^{0q} = \bigoplus_x H^q(\operatorname{Spec} R(X_x), F) \qquad \text{si } q > 0 , \] where \(x\) traverses the set of closed points of \(X\), and where \(X_x\) is the localized strict of \(X\) to the point \(x\).

The spectral sequence therefore gives an exact sequence \[ \begin{aligned} 0 \longrightarrow H^1(X, i_* F) &\longrightarrow H^1(\operatorname{Spec} R(X), F) \longrightarrow \bigoplus_x H^1(\operatorname{Spec} R(X_x), F) \\ \longrightarrow H^2(X, i_* F) &\longrightarrow H^2(\operatorname{Spec} R(X), F) \longrightarrow \bigoplus_x H^2(\operatorname{Spec} R(X_x), F) \\ &\longrightarrow \cdots . \end{aligned} \]

Let us now suppose that in addition the \(\ell\)-cohomological dimension of \(\operatorname{Spec} R(X)\), for a given prime number \(\ell\), or at most equal to \(1\) (i.e. we have \(H^q(\operatorname{Spec} R(X), F) = 0\), \(q > 1\), for each beam \(F\) from \(\ell\)-twist on \(\operatorname{Spec} R(X)\)). Let \(K^1, \ldots, K^m\) be the residual bodies of the Artinian ring \(R(X)\), i.e. the residual bodies of the maximal points from \(X\). Taking into account the dictionary (VIII 2.1) the \(\ell\)-cohomo-dimension logic of \(\operatorname{Spec} R(X)\) is \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell(\operatorname{Spec} R(X)) = \sup_i \{ \operatorname{cd}_\ell(G(\bar{K}^i/K^i)) \} , \] where \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell(G)\) is the \(\ell\)-cohomological dimension of the profinite group \(G\) [cf. CG].

Each residual body \(K_x\) of \(R(\bar{X}(x))\), for strict localization \(\bar{X}(x)\) of \(X\), obviously identifies with a separable (infinite) extension of one of \(K^i\), hence \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell(G(\bar{K}_x/K_x)) \leqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell(G(\bar{K}^i/K^i)) \] according to (CG II 4.1, Prop. 10). So the \(\ell\)-cohomological dimension \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell(\operatorname{Spec} R(\bar{X}(x)))\) is also \(\leqslant 1\).

By applying the exact sequence of 4.2 to a \(F\) of \(\ell\)-torsion, we find \[ (4.3.1) \qquad \left\{ \begin{aligned} &0 \to H^1(X, i_* F) \to H^1(\operatorname{Spec} R(X), F) \to \\ &\qquad \bigoplus_x H^1(\operatorname{Spec} R(\bar{X}(x)), F) \to H^2(X, i_* F) \to 0 , \\ &H^q(X, i_* F) = 0 \quad \text{pour } q > 2 . \end{aligned} \right. \]

Note that the hypothesis that \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell(\operatorname{Spec} R(X))\) is \(\leqslant 1\) is satisfied if \(X\) is an algebraic curve over a separably closed field. (Tsen's Theorem [6, th.4.3]). Similar exact sequences have been studied in this case by Ogg [2] and Šafarevič.

Suppose that \(\ell\) is invertible on \(X\) and take \(F = (\mathbb{G}_m)_{R(X)}\). Applying 3.3 and [CG, chap. I, prop. 12], we find, always under the hypothesis that \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell(\operatorname{Spec} R(X)) \leqslant 1\), \[ (4.4.1) \qquad \left\{ \begin{aligned} &H^1(\operatorname{Spec} R(X), \mathbb{G}_m) = 0 \\ &H^q(\operatorname{Spec} R(X), \mathbb{G}_m) \underset{\ell}{=} 0 \qquad q \geqslant 2 , \end{aligned} \right. \] where the \(\ell\) under the equality means that the group of the first member, which is of torsion in any case, has no \(\ell\)-torsion.

We have the same result if we replace \(R(X)\) by \(R(\bar{X}(x))\). Since the beam \(\mathbb{G}_m\) on \(\operatorname{Spec} R(X)\) obviously induces the beam \(\mathbb{G}_m\) on \(\operatorname{Spec} R(\bar{X}(x))\), we deduce from 4.2 \[ (4.4.2) \qquad \left\{ \begin{aligned} &H^1(X, i_*(\mathbb{G}_m)_{R(X)}) = 0 \\ &H^q(X, i_*(\mathbb{G}_m)_{R(X)}) \underset{\ell}{=} 0 , \qquad \text{si } q > 1 . \end{aligned} \right. \]

But for any beam \(F\) on \(X\), we see thanks to 4.1 that the kernel \(P\) and cokernel \(Q\) of the canonical homomorphism \(F \longrightarrow i_* i^* F\) are "sheaves skyscraper", therefore (like the residual bodies in the closed points of \(X\) are assumed to be separably closed) we have \(H^i(X, P) = H^i(X, Q) = 0\) for \(i > 0\), which immediately implies, by the exact cohomology sequence, that \[ H^i(X, F) \longrightarrow H^i(X, i_* i^* F) \] is an isomorphism for \(i \geqslant 2\). Taking into account 4.4.2 we conclude the relationship \[ (4.5) \qquad H^i(X, \mathbb{G}_m) \underset{\ell}{=} 0 \qquad \text{pour } i \geqslant 2 , \] valid when \(X\) is a Noetherian preschema of dimension \(1\), satisfying at \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell(R(X)) \leqslant 1\), and whose residual bodies at the closed points are separably closed. Considering Kummer's theory (3.2) and (3.3) we conclude

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.6

Let \(X\) noetherian of dimension \(1\), \(n\) an integer which is invertible on \(X\), suppose that \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell(R(X)) \leqslant 1\) for each number first \(\ell\) which divides \(n\), and that for any closed point \(x\) of \(X\), the body residual \(k(x)\) is separably closed. So we have \[ H^q(X, (\mu_n)_X) = 0 \quad \text{si } q > 2 , \] and the cohomology for \(q \leqslant 2\) is given by the exact sequence \[ \begin{aligned} 0 \longrightarrow H^0(X, (\mu_n)_X) &\longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}^*_X) \xrightarrow{\ n\ } \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}^*_X) \longrightarrow \\ H^1(X, (\mu_n)_X) &\longrightarrow \operatorname{Pic} X \xrightarrow{\ n\ } \operatorname{Pic} X \longrightarrow H^2(X, (\mu_n)_X) \longrightarrow 0 . \end{aligned} \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.7

Let \(X\) be a complete connected curve on a separate body bly closed \(k\), from primary characteristic to \(n\). So we have \[ H^0(X, (\mu_n)_X) \simeq \mu_n(k) , \] \[ H^1(X, (\mu_n)_X) \simeq {}_n(\operatorname{Pic} X) = \text{ensemble des éléments de } \operatorname{Pic} X \text{ dont l'ordre divise } n , \] \[ H^2(X, (\mu_n)_X) \simeq (\operatorname{Pic} X)/n \simeq (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^c , \] where \(c\) is the set of irreducible components of \(X\).

For the last assertion, "recall" that the "Picard diagram" [TDTE V] of such \(X/\operatorname{Spec} k\) admits a decomposition \[ (4.8) \qquad 0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow \operatorname{Pic}_{X/k} \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}^c \longrightarrow 0 \] where \(A\) is a connected algebraic group on \(\operatorname{Spec} k\). But the application "multiplication by \(n\)" in such a \(A\) is equal, therefore surjective for points with values in \(k\). It follows that we have \[ {}_n(\operatorname{Pic} X) \approx {}_n(A(k)) \] and \[ (\operatorname{Pic} X)/n \approx (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^c . \]

If \(X\) is smooth on \(\operatorname{Spec} k\), so that the Jacobian \(A\) is an abelian variety, the group \({}_n(\operatorname{Pic} X)\) is isomorphic to \((\mathbb{Z}/n)^{2g}\), where \(g\) is the type of \(X\). Of course, we also have \(\mu_n(k) \approx \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\), so the ranks of cohomology groups as \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-modules are \(1\), \(2g\), \(1\), which is the result we should have expected.
5. The trace method

Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(f : X' \longrightarrow X\) be a flat covering, and \(F\) be a beam to \(X\). Then the addition formulas give a morphism restriction \[ F \xrightarrow{\ \text{res}\ } f_* f^* F . \] As \(f\) is finite, we have \(H^q(X, f_* f^* F) \simeq H^q(X', f^* F)\) hence a morphism, also called restriction: \[ (5.1.1) \qquad H^q(X, F) \xrightarrow{\ \text{res}\ } H^q(X', f^* F) , \] which is also the obvious morphism. But in the case \(f\) we have also another morphism, called morphism trace \[ (5.1.2) \qquad f_* f^* F \xrightarrow{\ \text{tr}\ } F , \] which gives morphisms on cohomology \[ (5.1.3) \qquad H^q(X', f^* F) \xrightarrow{\ \text{tr}\ } H^q(X, F) . \] The trace morphism is characterized by the following two properties:
(i) It is local in character for the sparse topology.
(ii) If \(X'\) is disjoint sum of \(d\) copies of \(X\), so that \(f_* f^* F \simeq F^d\), the trace is the sum application \(F^d \longrightarrow F\).
Uniqueness is trivial from (i), (ii) since any covering The spread is locally constant. Having oneness, existence follows by the usual argument of descent.
From (ii) we deduce the following formula: \[ (5.1.4) \qquad \operatorname{tr}\circ\operatorname{res} : F \longrightarrow F \text{ est la multiplication par le degré local de } f . \]
If the degree is a constant \(d\), we deduce that the morphism \(\operatorname{tr}\circ\operatorname{res} : H^q(X, F) \longrightarrow H^q(X, F)\) is multiplication by \(d\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2

Let \(f : X' \longrightarrow X\) be a flat coating of constant degree \(d\). If \(F\) is a \(r\)-twist beam on \(X\) with \((r,d)=1\), then \(H^q(X', f^* F) = 0\) implies that \(H^q(X, F) = 0\).
Indeed, multiplication by \(d\) induces an isomorphism of \(F\), so an isomorphism on cohomology. If this last isomorphism is factorized by zero, we have \(H^q(X, F) = 0\).

Let us now be \(i : U \longrightarrow X\) an immersion, \(f : U' \longrightarrow U\) a slack covering of degree \(d\), and suppose \(f\) induced by a morphism finished \(g : X' \longrightarrow X\) by change of base, i.e. we then have the diagram Cartesian \[ \xymatrix{ U' \ar[r]^{i'} \ar[d]_{f} & X' \ar[d]^{g} \\ U \ar[r]_{i} & X } \] Let \(F\) be a beam on \(U\). We have \[ \xymatrix{ F \ar[r]^-{\text{res}} \ar@/_1.8pc/[rr]_{d} & f_* f^* F \ar[r]^-{\text{tr}} & F } , \] so, like \(i_* f_* = g_* i'_*\), \[ (5.3) \qquad \xymatrix{ i_* F \ar[r]^-{\text{res}} \ar@/_1.8pc/[rr]_{d} & g_* i'_* f^* F \ar[r]^-{\text{tr}} & i_* F } , \] and like \(i_! f_! \simeq g_* i'_!\) (results e.g. from (VIII 5.5)), \[ (5.4) \qquad \xymatrix{ i_! F \ar[r]^-{\text{res}} \ar@/_1.8pc/[rr]_{d} & g_* i'_! f^* F \ar[r]^-{\text{tr}} & i_! F } , \] hence \[ (5.3') \qquad \xymatrix{ H^q(X, i_* F) \ar[r]^-{\text{res}} \ar@/_1.8pc/[rr]_{d'} & H^q(X', i'_* f^* F) \ar[r]^-{\text{tr}} & H^q(X, i_* F) } \] and \[ (5.4') \qquad \xymatrix{ H^q(X, i_! F) \ar[r]^-{\text{res}} \ar@/_1.8pc/[rr]_{d} & H^q(X', i'_! f^* F) \ar[r]^-{\text{tr}} & H^q(X, i_! F) } ; \]
We deduce from (5.4) the following corollaries:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.5

Let \(X\) a quasi-compact and quasi-separate scheme, \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\), and let \(H\) a semi-exact functor with values in \((\mathrm{Ab})\), defined on the category of \(\ell\)-beams on \(X\), and compatible with the limits filtering inductive. The following assertions are equivalent:

(i) \(H = 0\).

(ii) \(H(f_* i_!(\mathbb{Z}/\ell)) = 0\) for each \(f : Y \longrightarrow X\) finished, and each open \(i : U \longrightarrow Y\) of \(Y\), such that \(U\) is of finite presentation on \(X\). If \(X\) is Noetherian, we can even limit ourselves to taking \(Y\) integrity.

Demonstration. According to 2.7.2 and 2.5 just check \(H(F) = 0\) for \(F\) of the form \(F = i_! G\), where \(i : U \longrightarrow X\) is the inclusion of a subschema of \(X\) of finite presentation on \(X\), and where \(G\) is a \(\ell\) locally constant "isotrivial" beam on \(U\). Let \(U'' \longrightarrow U\) be a main covering, of group \(g\); such that \(g\) becomes constant on \(U''\). Let \(A\) be the \(\ell\)-group of the values ​​of \(G\) on \(U''\). The group \(g\) operates on \(A\), and this operation determines the structure of \(G\). Let \(h\) be a \(\ell\)-Sylow subgroup of \(g\), and let \(U' = U''/h\) be the covering of \(U\) corresponding to \(h\). The degree \(d\) of \(U'\) over \(U\) is prime to \(\ell\). Let us take an extension of \(f : U' \longrightarrow U\) into a finite morphism \(g : X' \longrightarrow X\), which still exists (EGA IV 8.12.6), and let \(i' : U' \longrightarrow X'\) be the inclusion. By applying 5.4 and the fact that \(d\) is prime to \(\ell\), we reduce to demonstrate that \(H(g_* i'_! f^* G) = 0\).

Now it is well known that the only abelian group of \(\ell\)-torsion which is simple for an operation of a \(\ell\)-group \(h\) is \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell\) with operation trivial, and it follows that there exists a filtration of \(A\) as \(h\)-module, whose successive quotients are isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell\) with oper- trivial ration. This filtration gives a corresponding filtration of \(f^* G\), and it is therefore sufficient, to demonstrate that \(H(g_* i'_! f^* G) = 0\) to demonstrate than \(H(g_* i'_! \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0\), hence the result.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.6

Let \(X\) a Noetherian schema, \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\), and \(\varphi\) a function with values in \(\mathbb{N}\), defined on the set of integrity schemes end up on \(X\). Assume \(\varphi(Y_1) \leqslant \varphi(Y_2)\) whenever it exists a \(X\)-morphism \(Y_1 \longrightarrow Y_2\), and that the inequality is strict if \(Y_1\) is a closed subschema of \(Y_2\), distinct from \(Y_2\). For all \(Y\) finished on \(X\), let \(\varphi(Y) = \sup_i \varphi(Y_i)\), where the \(Y_i\) are the irreducible components of \(Y\), equipped with the reduced induced structure. Then the following assertions are equivalent:

(i) For each \(Y\) finitely on \(X\) and each \(\ell\)-beam \(F\) on \(Y\) we have \(H^q(Y, F) = 0\) if \(q > \varphi(\operatorname{Supp} F)\).

(ii) For each \(Y\) finite integrator on \(X\) we have \(H^q(Y, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0\) if \(q > \varphi(Y)\).

Demonstration. Suppose (ii) is true. We can apply the previous proposition at each \(H^q\), taking into account that the cohomology commutes to inductive limits and direct images by finite morphisms, and we thus reduce ourselves for the verification of (i) to the case \(Y\) integrates ended on \(X\), and \(F\) of the form \(i_!(\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_U\), where \(i : U \longrightarrow X\) is an open non-empty.

But we have an exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow i_!(\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_U \longrightarrow (\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_Y \longrightarrow (\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_Z \longrightarrow 0 \] where \(Z = Y-U\) therefore \(Z \neq Y\). Performing an induction on the value of \(\varphi\), we we assume that \(H^q(Z, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0\) for \(q > \varphi(Y)-1\), hence \(H^q(Y, i_!(\mathbb{Z}/\ell)) = 0\) for \(q > \varphi(Y)\) thanks to the exact sequence of coho- mology.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.7

Let \(X\) an algebraic curve on a body \(k\) separa- bly closed and with characteristics different from \(\ell\), \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\). So the coho- mology of \(X\) in a beam \(F\) of \(\ell\)-torsion is zero in dimension \(> 2\). If \(X\) is affine, the cohomology is zero in dimension \(> 1\).

Let, for \(Y\) finite integrator be \(X\), \(\varphi(Y) = 2 \dim Y\) (resp. \(\varphi(Y) = \dim Y\) if \(X\), then \(Y\), is affine). According to 5.6 it is enough to prove that \(H^q(Y, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0\) if \(q > \varphi(Y)\), But it is trivial if \(\dim Y = 0\). It therefore remains to demonstrate that \(H^q(Y, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0\) if \(q > 2\) (resp. if \(q > 1\) in the affine case) for an integral curve \(Y\). Since \((\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_Y \simeq (\mu_\ell)_Y\), this is a consequence of 4.6 for \(q > 2\).

Assume \(X\) affines. Since root extensions do not affect not cohomology (VIII 1.1), we reduce to the case where \(k\) is algebraically closed. Let \(f : X' \longrightarrow X\) be the normalization of \(X\), which is a morphism finite and an isomorphism outside of a finite set of points, say \(S\). We have an exact sequence of beams on \(X\) \[ 0 \longrightarrow (\mathbb{Z}/n)_X \longrightarrow f_*(\mathbb{Z}/n)_{X'} \longrightarrow \mathscr{E} \longrightarrow 0 , \] where \(\mathscr{E}\) is focused on \(S\), so \(H^q(X, \mathscr{E}) = 0\) for \(q > 0\). We are reduced by (VIII 5.6) to demonstrate that \[ H^q(X, f_*(\mathbb{Z}/n)_{X'}) \simeq H^q(X', \mathbb{Z}/n) = 0 \] if \(q > 1\), that is to say, in case \(X\) normal. We can also obviously assume \(X\) related. Let \(i : X \longrightarrow \bar{X}\) be an open immersion with complete \(\bar{X}\) and non-singular. Since \(X\) is affine, it is missing at least one point of \(\bar{X}\), and we immediately see that as a result the morphism \[ A(k) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Pic} X \] is surjective, where \(A\) is the Jacobian of \(\bar{X}\) (cf. 4.8). Since \(A(k)\) is divisible by \(\ell \neq \operatorname{car} k\), the same is true of \(\operatorname{Pic} X\), hence \(H^2(X, \mu_\ell) = 0\) by 4.6, cqfd.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 5.8

Let us point out, for future reference, that the reasoning of 5.5 in fact establishes the following result: Let \(X\) be a quasi- compact and quasi-separated, \(\ell\) a prime number, \(C\) a strictly subcategory full of the category of constructible \(\ell\)-torsion abelian sheaves, stable by direct factors and by extensions. Suppose that for each finite morphism \(f : Y \longrightarrow X\) and each open \(i : U \longrightarrow Y\) of \(Y\) which is presentation finished on \(X\), we have \(f_*(i_!(\underline{\mathbb{Z}/\ell\,\mathbb{Z}})_U) \in C\). Then \(C\) contains all constructible beams of \(\ell\)-torsion.
